statement.markdown:
Given an array of integers, sort the array using recursive insertion sort.

Insertion sort is a simple sorting algorithm that works much like the way you sort playing cards in your hands. The array is virtually split into a sorted and an unsorted region. Each subsequent element from the unsorted region is inserted into the sorted region in its correct position.

The goal is to implement this using recursion.

For example, if the input array is `[4, 2, 7, 1, 3]`, the output should be `[1, 2, 3, 4, 7]`.

examples.explanation: The array is sorted using recursive insertion sort.
The array is sorted using recursive insertion sort.

hints.text: To implement recursive insertion sort, think about how you can divide the problem into smaller sub-problems. Consider what happens when you have a sorted portion of the array and an unsorted portion.

The base case for your recursion could be when the array has one or zero elements. For larger arrays, consider how you can insert an element from the unsorted portion into the sorted portion. Think about shifting elements to make room for the insertion.

The key to solving this problem recursively is to recognize that the recursive call can sort the unsorted portion of the array (minus one element), and then you insert that one element into the sorted portion. Consider how you can use a loop to shift elements in the sorted portion to make room for the insertion.

editorial.title: Recursive Insertion Sort Analysis
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by recursively sorting the array and then inserting each element into its correct position. The key observation is that insertion sort is efficient for nearly sorted arrays, and a recursive approach can be used to sort the array in a top-down manner.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would be to use a simple recursive sorting algorithm such as recursive bubble sort or recursive selection sort, but these would have a time complexity of O(n^3) or worse. The given recursive insertion sort has a time complexity of O(n^2) which is much better.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach is to use a divide-and-conquer algorithm or a more efficient sorting algorithm such as quicksort or mergesort. However, for this problem, the recursive insertion sort is the given solution.
editorial.optimalSolution: The given recursive insertion sort solution has a time complexity of O(n^2) and a space complexity of O(n) due to the recursive call stack.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the recursive insertion sort can be proven by induction. The base case is when n = 1, the array is already sorted. For the inductive step, assume that the array is sorted up to n-1 elements, and then the nth element is inserted into its correct position. This process maintains the sorted property of the array.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm with an example array [5, 2, 8, 3, 1] shows that the recursive calls sort the array up to the second last element, and then the last element is inserted into its correct position.
commonMistakes: Not considering the base case of the recursion correctly
Not handling the edge case of an empty array
Using a different sorting algorithm that is not suitable for this problem
Not considering the time and space complexity of the algorithm
Not testing the algorithm with edge cases and large inputs
